---
title: "Procedure"
---

[← Back to Lab 2 overview](index.qmd)

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Working draft.** Procedure is being refined as the lab runs this quarter; expect updates day-to-day.
:::

- The lab collects **six step-response datasets** per team — three parameter configurations (Baseline, Feedforward, Tuned) × two control channels (speed, yaw-rate).
- The same field workflow runs three times; only the parameter set changes between cases.
- **Quality is checked at the field** before each parameter-set change, so problems are caught while the boat is still in the water.

## 1. Pre-lab preparation

### 1.1 Day before

- Collect: Boat, 900 MHz radio, R/C transmitter, ground laptop (with Mission Planner).
- Charge: Batteries for vehicle propulsion, autopilot, R/C transmitter, ground laptop.

### 1.2 Day of

- **Test card / data sheet printed** — one row per dataset (six total) for parameter values, observations, and quality-check verdict.
- **Parameter files staged on the laptop.** You are given the **baseline** `.param` for experiment A. 
- **Roles:**
  - **Planner** — drives Mission Planner; 
  - **Scribe** — keeps the test card; records UTC time and intent at every arming event.
  - **Pilot** — drives the boat with the RC transmitter; executes the step inputs.
## 2. Shore setup

Steps to confirm at the launch site, before any data collection.

- Establish telemetry link with Mission Planner 
- Load the baseline `.param` file and confirm the relevant `ATC_*` parameters are at the expected values. % CLAUDE: Add link to our documentation in introduction-to-feedback-control/site/autopilot/stabilization.qmd
- Confirm you can ARM and switch from MANUAL to ACRO modes.
- You may need to re-bind the RC transmitter and/or calibrate sensors.

## 3. Experiment execution

The lab cycles through three experiments — A (Baseline), B (Feedforward), C (Tuned) — applying the same field workflow to each. Two conventions span all three: per-arm logging (§3.1) and the per-experiment numbered workflow (§3.2).

### 3.1 Per-arm rhythm and logging

The autopilot starts a **new log file every time the pilot arms it**. Treat each experiment as a arm-disarm cycle.

The recommended process for making sense of the resulting logs is:
- **Setup** - Pilot moves USV to open water.
- **Start: De-ARM and Re-ARM** — scribe records UTC time (with seconds) and a brief description of the intent (e.g. *"Baseline, yaw-rate step, run #2"*). When the `.BIN` is post-processed to `.mat`, the UTC timestamp is appended to the filename — the scribe's notes are how you map a `.mat` back to what was being tested.
- **Before the step** — pilot switches the autopilot into ACRO mode (engaging the feedback controllers). The planner confirms ACRO mode in Mission Planner *before* the pilot executes the test.
- **After the step or step series** — pilot disarms and re-arms to close the run. This keeps each `.mat` file scoped to a single experimental purpose, which makes the MATLAB time-series easier to inspect.
- Expect to accumulate dozens of `.mat` files over a session. The scribe's log is what makes them navigable.

### 3.2 Per-experiment workflow

For each of the three experiments (A → B → C):

1. **Change the parameter set** for this experiment via Mission Planner (Config → Full Parameter Tree).  For the first experiment, load the baseline `.param` file. For the next two experiments, edit the baseline  to create the feedforward and tuned versions, then load those.
2. **Verify the relevant `ATC_*` parameters** at the vehicle. Mission Planner shows live values — see the [Stabilization Layer](../../autopilot/stabilization.qmd) reference for what each parameter does and which ones change between experiments.
3. **Speed step responses** — §4.1 for the technique.
4. **Yaw-rate step responses** — §4.2 for the technique.
5. **Field quality check** — pull the log, plot the candidate steps, decide keep / repeat (§5).
6. **Save and label** — note the log filename and parameter values on the test card.

### 3.3 What changes between experiments

| Experiment | Parameter changes from previous | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Baseline** | Lab preset configuration; ArduRover stock PID gains; FF off; rate limiters off; throttle baseline off | First case — fully load the lab baseline `.param` |
| **B — Feedforward** | Restore `ATC_SPEED_FF` and `ATC_STR_RAT_FF` to lab values | Same PID gains as A |
| **C — Tuned** | Adjust `ATC_SPEED_P/I/D` and `ATC_STR_RAT_P/I/D` for best response | FF kept on |

## 4. Step-input technique

Manual step inputs from the RC stick. The pilot approximates an instantaneous step change in the setpoint, then holds it long enough to capture both the transient and the settling behavior.

### 4.1 Speed (throttle) steps

- **Start at rest** on open water.
- Apply a step throttle command at **~50% of full stick deflection.** A mid-range step keeps the actuator out of saturation while still being large enough to be visible above noise.
- **Hold for 20–30 s** so the surge response settles into a clear steady value.
- **Repeat 3–5 times** per dataset to average across pilot timing variability.
- Keep the throttle stick at zero between repeats long enough for the boat to come back to rest.
- Disarm at the end of the speed trials and re-arm for the yaw-rate trials so each log file holds one channel only.

### 4.2 Yaw-rate (steering) steps

- **The boat must be moving** before the step. The rudder needs forward water flow to produce a turning moment — at zero surge speed, a steering command does nothing useful. Hold a low, approximately constant throttle (set the surge first, let it settle, then step the steering).
- Apply a step steering command and **hold until the yaw rate settles** to a steady value.
- Capture **both directions** (positive and negative stick deflection) so any asymmetry in the response is visible.
- The scribe records the surge speed at the time of each step, the step fraction, and the direction.
- Use the same step fraction across all three experiments so the A/B/C metrics are directly comparable.

### 4.3 Common gotchas

- **Don't measure both channels in the same arming cycle.** Keep speed and yaw step trials in separate log files — easier to align in MATLAB and prevents accidental cross-coupling in the data.
- **Inter-step gap too short.** Allow the response to fully settle (return to baseline or steady value) before the next step. A short gap mixes one step's tail into the next step's start.
- **Saturation.** If the control effort pins at ±1 (full PWM) for an extended period, what you're measuring is the *saturated* dynamics, not the linear closed-loop response. Either drop the step magnitude or, in experiment C, increase control authority through tuning.
- **Same step fraction across A / B / C.** Comparing rise time, overshoot, and steady-state error across experiments only makes sense if the input is the same.

## 5. Quality check (before leaving the field)

Run the visual inspection on the laptop while the boat is still launched, so any failed dataset can be recollected on the spot.

1. Pull the latest `.BIN` log from the autopilot SD card or via Mission Planner.
2. Run [`bin2mat.py`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/utils/ardu_utils/bin2mat.py) to convert: produces a `.mat` file alongside the `.BIN`.
3. Run [`proc_closedloop_response.m`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/proc_closedloop_response.m) in MATLAB to plot the timeseries.
4. Check each step in the figure:
   - **Setpoint reaches the commanded value** (no rate-limited ramp instead of a step)
   - **Measured response settles** to a steady value within ~10 s
   - **Control effort is not pinned** at ±1 for an extended period (saturation)
   - **PIDT / PIDS log channels are present** (PID-internal logging is on)
   - **No long gaps** in the GPS or STER sample stream

If any check fails for the dataset just collected → recollect before moving on. If it's a parameter or vehicle issue → resolve, then continue.

## 6. End-of-day

Wrap up the field session.

- All six `.mat` files transferred to the team's shared location.
- Test card filled in (one row per dataset, with quality-check verdict).
- Vehicle returned to nominal parameter configuration (rate limiters restored, FF restored, throttle baseline restored) — load a `.param` file with the post-lab nominal values.
- Battery on charger, vehicle stowed.

## 7. Hand-off to next week

Datasets and notes are the input to the Week-8 analysis: process each `.mat` with [`closedloop_stepinfo.m`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/closedloop_stepinfo.m) to extract rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error, then compare across the three experiments.
